mappings.rationaleAI: The Big Bang theory, Hubble's expansion discovery, cosmic microwave background radiation, nucleosynthesis, and inflation theory comprehensively address large-scale structure (A.1.b), age of the universe (A.1.c), CMB (A.1.e), and the origin and development of the universe (E.1).

Dark matter constituting 27% of mass-energy, evidence from galaxy rotation curves and gravitational lensing, and its role in large-scale structure formation address the missing mass problem (A.1.f) and clustering of galaxies (A.1.d).
Dark energy driving accelerating cosmic expansion, the cosmological constant, and implications for the universe's ultimate fate address the expanding universe (A.1.b) and cosmological models (E.1).

Efforts to determine the universe's age at 13.8 billion years using Hubble's constant, CMB measurements, and Type Ia supernovae directly address the age of the universe (A.1.c) and time scale dating (E.3).
Galaxy classification, formation, evolution, supermassive black holes, and the Milky Way's structure address clustering of galaxies (A.1.d), distant galaxies (A.3.a), and origin of galaxies (E.2.a).

Einstein's relativity covering spacetime as a four-dimensional continuum, gravity as spacetime curvature, and experimental confirmations directly address space-time (A.1.g), special relativity (D.1), and general relativity (D.2).

Black holes from general relativity, Schwarzschild solutions, Hawking radiation, and supermassive black holes at galactic centers address quasars and hypothetical supermassive black holes (A.3.c) and general relativity implications (D.2).
Gravitational waves predicted by general relativity and detected via LIGO directly address experimental confirmation of general relativity (D.2.b).
The life cycle of stars from formation through nucleosynthesis addresses formation and development of stars (E.2.b) and stars as components of the universe (A.3.e).
Einstein's general relativity reconceiving gravity as spacetime curvature and experimental confirmations address modern gravitational theory (B.3) and properties of the space-time continuum (D).
The multiverse hypothesis including many-worlds interpretation and eternal inflation addresses cosmological models as theoretical representations of the universe's behaviour (A.2).
The Michelson-Morley experiment disproving the aether and Einstein's postulate of c as invariant address the historical search for the ether (D.1.a) and relativity of space and time (D.1.b).
The baryon asymmetry problem (why matter dominates over antimatter) and CP violation address extrapolations backward in time to the beginning of the universe (E.1.c).
Newton's law of universal gravitation as a foundational concept in gravitational theory addresses the development of gravitational theory (B.1.b).
Infrared and submillimeter astronomy studying cosmic microwave background, cold molecular clouds, and star formation addresses CMB (A.1.e), nebulae (A.3.d), and star formation (E.2.b).
Eddington's 1919 eclipse expedition confirming general relativity's prediction of light bending directly addresses experimental confirmation of general relativity (D.2.b).
